title: Listening - Part 4: Long-Distance Running
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five individuals discussing their involvement in marathon and ultra-marathon running

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d always been a bit of a couch potato, but [21]I took up running primarily because I wanted to raise a significant amount of money for the hospice that cared for my father[/21]. I needed a big goal. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the massive improvement in my sleep quality[/26]. I used to struggle with insomnia for years, but now I’m so physically tired that I drop off instantly. It’s transformed my energy levels during the day and made me much more productive at work. || Speaker 2: I’m a surgeon, and [22]I started long-distance running because I needed a way to manage the extreme stress and high stakes of my professional life[/22]. I needed a pressure valve. [27]What I found most difficult, though, was the initial frustration of how slow my progress was[/27]. I’m used to being good at things, and being out of breath after just one mile was very humbling. I had to learn to park my ego and just put in the work, which was a valuable lesson in patience that I’ve since applied to my medical practice. || Speaker 3: I recently moved to a new country and [23]I joined a running club because I wanted to meet people and find a community where I felt I belonged[/23]. I was feeling quite isolated. [28]The most rewarding aspect has been the deep and lasting friendships I’ve formed during those long, slow training runs[/28]. You can really get to know someone when you’re running together for three hours. It’s given me a social support system that I never would have found otherwise. || Speaker 4: I’m in my fifties, and [24]I started ultra-marathons because I wanted to challenge the societal narrative that our bodies inevitably break down as we get older[/24]. I wanted to see what I was still capable of. [29]The biggest hurdle has been the constant battle with minor injuries, like shin splints and blisters[/29]. My recovery time is definitely longer than it used to be. I’ve had to become an expert in nutrition and stretching just to stay on the road. It’s a full-time job in itself. || Speaker 5: I’d always struggled with a lack of self-discipline, so [25]I signed up for a marathon because I wanted to prove to myself that I could stick to a strict, long-term plan[/25]. I wanted to build my character. [30]The most significant outcome has been the massive boost in my overall mental resilience[/30]. Now, when I face a challenge in my personal life, I just tell myself 'you ran 26 miles, you can handle this'. It’s given me a sense of inner strength and confidence that has changed the way I look at every other obstacle in my path.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to start running.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to raise money for a hospice that cared for her father.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 needed a way to manage the extreme stress of his job as a surgeon.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to meet people and find a community after moving to a new country.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to challenge the idea that bodies break down with age.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to prove she could stick to a strict, long-term plan and build discipline.

options.A: to win a bet with a colleague
options.B: to test their physical limits in later life
options.C: to prepare for a different sporting event
options.D: to follow a medical professional's advice
options.G: to develop a more disciplined personality
options.H: to expand their social circle in a new area

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as a result of their running.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a massive improvement in her sleep quality.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 found the slow pace of initial progress very humbling and frustrating.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 highlights the deep and lasting friendships formed during training.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the constant battle with minor injuries and recovery time.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 mentions a boost in mental resilience and inner strength.

options.A: a change in their dietary habits
options.B: an increased sense of psychological toughness
options.C: the pleasure of winning a race
options.D: an unexpected improvement in their rest
options.E: the high cost of specialized footwear
options.F: the frustration of recurring physical ailments
options.G: struggling with a lack of immediate success
options.H: the value of the friendships they developed
